# Ninad Pathak

> Programmer-turned-writer helping DevTools and SaaS companies create technical content their engineering teams actually approve.

Technical writer and former engineer publishing in-depth, benchmark-backed writing on AI agents, agent memory, RAG, LLM inference, and technical content strategy for DevTools and B2B SaaS. Articles below are grouped by topic; each links to the canonical URL.

## Topic Hubs

- [AI Agent Architecture](https://ninadpathak.com/ai-agent-architecture/): The patterns, trade-offs, and failure modes behind production AI agents. From the agent loop and tool calls to multi-agent design and error handling.
- [AI Agent Memory](https://ninadpathak.com/ai-agent-memory/): How production AI agents store, retrieve, and forget information. A practitioner's guide to memory architecture, from context windows to episodic recall, backed by benchmarks.
- [AI Workflows & Automation](https://ninadpathak.com/ai-workflows/): How to wire AI agents and coding assistants into real work: choosing models, setting up coding agents, connecting tools with MCP, and automating the busywork without losing control.
- [LLM Inference & Cost](https://ninadpathak.com/llm-inference/): How LLMs actually run, and how to make them faster and cheaper. Context windows, token budgets, prompt caching, speculative decoding, TTFT, and KV-cache internals, all benchmarked.
- [RAG & Retrieval](https://ninadpathak.com/rag/): A practitioner's guide to retrieval-augmented generation: embeddings, hybrid search, reranking, evaluation, and the optimizations that decide whether your RAG system works.
- [Technical Writing for DevTools](https://ninadpathak.com/technical-writing/): How to write documentation, tutorials, and developer content that engineers respect and buyers act on. The craft, the business case, and what separates practitioner writing from marketing.

## AI Agent Memory

- [Building a Customer Support Agent with Persistent Memory: A Worked Example](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/agent-memory-for-customer-support/): I built a customer support agent that actually remembers across sessions. Here is what I learned about memory architecture, serialization trade-offs, and the failure modes that will kill your deployment.
- [Why Your Coding Agent Keeps Forgetting Everything: Memory Persistence in AI Coding Assistants](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/why-coding-agents-lose-their-memory/): The memory persistence patterns that actually work for AI coding assistants, and why most agents lose context between sessions.
- [Memory for Voice AI Agents: What Text Chatbots Cannot Do](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/memory-for-voice-ai-agents/): Voice AI agents live or die by how they manage memory across a real-time streaming pipeline. Text chatbots solve memory with RAG. Voice agents need something different.
- [State of AI Agent Memory in 2026](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/state-of-ai-agent-memory-2026/): The memory stack for AI agents has exploded into a fragmented mess of competing approaches. Here is what actually works, what is still research, and why the next 18 months will sort the winners from the wreckage.
- [Context Windows vs Memory: Why They Are Not the Same Thing](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/context-windows-vs-memory/): A 1M token context window is not memory. Treating it like one is how you build expensive systems that still forget what they were doing last Tuesday.
- [How Memory Works in HyperAgents](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/how-memory-works-in-hyperagents/): A deep dive into how HyperAgents retain context across interactions, layer memory architectures, and handle session continuity in production.
- [The BEAM Memory Benchmark: Why 1M Context Windows Are Not Enough](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/beam-memory-benchmark/): The BEAM benchmark reveals that LLMs fail catastrophically at retrieving facts from the middle of long contexts. Here is what the data actually shows, why it happens, and what matters for real deployments.
- [How Memory Works in DeerFlow](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/how-memory-works-in-deerflow/): A deep dive into the memory architecture of DeerFlow: layered context passing, session state files, sub-agent isolation, and how it compares to Letta, AutoGen, and CrewAI.
- [Memory Hierarchy in AI Systems: From Sensory to Semantic](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/memory-hierarchy-in-ai-systems/): How layered memory architecture helps AI systems achieve long-term context, personalization, and continuous learning, and why flat memory fails.
- [How Memory Works in Claude Code](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/how-memory-works-in-claude-code/): A practical guide to understanding how Claude Code retains context across sessions, uses project files, and manages long-term memory for coding tasks.
- [RAG vs Memory: What AI Developers Need to Know](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/rag-vs-memory/): Understand the fundamental differences between RAG and memory systems for LLM applications, when to use each, and how to combine them in production.
- [AI Memory Management for LLMs: What Actually Works](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/ai-memory-management-for-llms/): A senior engineer's breakdown of what memory management for LLMs actually looks like in production: eviction strategies, KV cache management, importance-weighted retention, and why your agent keeps forgetting things.
- [Short-Term Memory for AI Agents: A Practical Guide](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/short-term-memory-for-ai-agents/): Context windows are not memory. Here is what every production AI agent engineer needs to understand about token budgets, overflow handling, and how short-term and long-term memory actually work together.

## Agent Architecture

- [The Agentic Workflow Playbook: From Prompt to Shipped PR](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/agentic-workflow-playbook/): The repeatable five-stage process I run to take a task from vague intent to a pull request I trust, with an AI agent doing the heavy lifting and me in the loop where being wrong is expensive.
- [How I Set Up a Coding Agent That Actually Finishes the Task](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/coding-agent-setup-that-works/): A coding agent succeeds or fails before it writes a line. Here is the setup I use, the rules file, repository context, tools, permissions, and guardrails, that turns a capable model into an agent I trust with real work.
- [Wiring Tools into Your Agent with MCP: A Practical Setup](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/mcp-server-setup-guide/): A hands-on guide to connecting an AI agent to your own tools and data with the Model Context Protocol. The client-server shape, a minimal working server, tool design, and the guardrails that keep it safe.
- [The Agent Design Space: A Map of What Engineers Are Actually Building](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/the-agent-design-space/): After surveying production agents across industries, the design space clusters into patterns. Here is what I found.
- [A Taxonomy of AI Agents That Actually Explains What You Are Building](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/the-taxonomy-of-ai-agents/): Most AI agent taxonomies are either too academic or too vague to be useful. Here is the classification I use when I need to decide what kind of agent to build.
- [The State of Open Source AI Memory in 2026: Beyond the Context Window Myth](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/state-of-open-source-memory-2026/): AI architecture has reached a plateau in model reasoning. The next frontier of differentiation lives in stateful memory systems that solve identity fragmentation at production scale.
- [The Model Context Protocol (MCP) Explained: A Universal Language for AI Tools](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/model-context-protocol-explained/): Model Context Protocol (MCP) is the new standard for connecting AI models to data sources and tools. Here is why it matters, how it works, and why it is the missing link for agentic infrastructure.
- [Agent Harnesses: the Infrastructure Layer Your Llm Agent Actually Needs](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/agent-harnesses/): Every production AI agent needs a harness. Here is what one contains, why frameworks often are not enough, and how to build the layer that actually determines reliability.

## RAG & Retrieval

- [Fine-Tuning vs RAG for Agent Memory: When Each Approach Makes Sense](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/fine-tuning-vs-rag-for-agent-memory/): Fine-tuning and RAG solve different parts of the agent memory problem. Here is how to decide which one you actually need.
- [The Memory Hierarchy: Why RAG Alone Is Not Enough for Agent Memory](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/the-memory-hierarchy-why-rag-is-not-enough/): RAG handles document retrieval well. It handles agent memory poorly, because agents need episodic recall, working context, and cross-session persistence that a vector store cannot provide.
- [Why Agent Memory Retrieval Is Asymmetric and Why It Breaks Your RAG Pipeline](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/asymmetric-retrieval-agent-memory/): The retrieval patterns that work for a query do not work when the agent is the one retrieving, and this asymmetry silently breaks production RAG systems.
- [RAG Evaluation Metrics: What Actually Matters](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/rag-evaluation-metrics-what-actually-matters/): A practical guide to RAGAs, recall, precision, and the metrics that separate production RAG systems from prototypes.
- [Hybrid Search: Combining Bm25 and Vector Search for Better Retrieval](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/hybrid-search-bm25-vector-search/): Hybrid search combines BM25 sparse retrieval with dense vector search. Here's how reciprocal rank fusion works, what it costs, and when the combination actually beats either method alone.
- [Semantic Caching: The RAG Optimization Nobody Talks About](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/semantic-caching-rag-optimization/): Semantic caching returns cached LLM responses for semantically similar queries, cutting API costs by 40-70% on the right workloads. Here's how the mechanism works and where it fails.
- [How Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval Changes RAG Architecture](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/how-anthropics-contextual-retrieval-changes-rag-architecture/): Anthropic says Contextual Retrieval cut top-20 retrieval failure by 49% with contextual embeddings plus contextual BM25. I walk through the mechanism, the benchmark, and the part of the RAG pipeline it changes.
- [Reranking in RAG: Why Your Top-K Results Are Probably Wrong](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/reranking-in-rag-why-your-top-k-results-are-probably-wrong/): Vector databases return results based on semantic similarity. I explain why that is rarely enough for production RAG and how a cross-encoder reranker fixes the problem.
- [Vector Embeddings: a Guide to the Geometry of Meaning in Ai](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/embedding-models-compared/): Everything in AI starts with a vector. Here is how embedding models turn human language into high-dimensional geometry, why dimensionality reduction matters, and how to choose between OpenAI, Cohere, and self-hosted models.
- [RAG vs Fine-Tuning: A Better Decision Framework](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/rag-vs-fine-tuning/): Choosing between Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) and Fine-Tuning is the most common architectural mistake in AI. Here is how to decide based on knowledge frequency, data privacy, and behavior requirements.

## LLM Inference, Cost & Internals

- [LLM Inference Optimization: What Actually Works in Production](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/llm-inference-optimization/): A practical breakdown of the inference optimization techniques that move the needle — batching, quantization, caching, and attention kernels — with concrete numbers and the tradeoffs between them.
- [LLM token budgets: a practical guide to cost control](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/llm-token-budgets-cost-control/): Real numbers, real pricing, and concrete strategies for keeping your LLM spend predictable.
- [Context Engineering as Heap Management: Measuring Accuracy vs. KV Cache Eviction](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/kv-cache-eviction-accuracy/): VRAM is too expensive to waste on low-attention tokens. I benchmarked KV cache eviction strategies to treat LLM context like a managed heap, reaching 90% pruning with zero recall loss.
- [Structured Outputs with LLMs: JSON Mode, Function Calling, and When to Use Each](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/structured-outputs-llms-json-mode-function-calling/): JSON mode, function calling, and structured outputs solve different problems. Here's when each one actually makes sense and what they cost you.
- [Token Counting Isn't Optional: a Practical Guide to Llm Cost Control](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/token-counting-isnt-optional-a-practical-guide-to-llm-cost-control/): I explain the mechanics of LLM tokenization, why JSON burns your API budget, and how to architect systems for strict token efficiency.
- [Llm Context Windows Explained: Why More Is Not Always Better](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/llm-context-windows-explained/): Context windows are expanding to millions of tokens. Here is why the middle of your context still gets ignored, what long context actually costs, and how to build production systems that use these massive windows effectively.
- [Mixture of Experts: How Moe Models Are Cheap to Run but Expensive to Host](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/mixture-of-experts-explained/): DeepSeek V3 has 671B parameters but only activates 37B per token. Here's how mixture of experts works, why it cuts inference costs, and the catch nobody puts in the headline.
- [Prompt Caching: What It Is and When the Math Works](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/prompt-caching-what-it-is-and-when-the-math-works/): Prompt caching can reduce LLM costs by up to 90% and cut latency by half. Here is the engineering guide to how it works, why prefix matching matters, and how to calculate your ROI.
- [The Best LLMs for Coding in 2026: An Engineering Review](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/best-llms-for-coding/): Not all models are created equal for software development. Here is a benchmark-backed guide to choosing the right LLM for autonomous agents, algorithmic logic, and repository-scale refactoring as of March 2026.
- [Time to First Token (TTFT): The Metric That Determines AI Snappiness](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/time-to-first-token-ttft/): Users do not care about total throughput. They care about how fast the first word appears. Here is the engineering guide to measuring and optimizing Time to First Token (TTFT) in production.
- [Speculative Decoding: How to Speed up Llm Inference for Free](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/speculative-decoding-explained/): LLM inference is memory-bound, not compute-bound. Speculative decoding uses this fact to speed up generation by 2-3x using a smaller draft model to predict tokens for a larger one.

## Technical Writing for DevTools

- [Technical Writing for Engineers: The 80/20 Guide](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/technical-writing-for-engineers/): Most engineering documentation fails for the same reasons. Here is what actually moves the needle.
- [What Nobody Tells You About Error Handling in Production AI Agents](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/production-ai-agent-errors/): Hard-won lessons from running AI agents in production: the error patterns that actually break systems, and the patterns that fix them.
- [From 4 Minutes to 12 Seconds: A Quantitative Audit of the uv Package Manager](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/uv-package-manager-benchmark/): I benchmarked Astral uv against Poetry and pip on a 2026 AI dependency stack. Here is how Rust-native tooling reduces build times by 20x.
- [Quantifying the Engineering Velocity Impact of Technical Documentation](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/engineering-velocity-documentation/): I analyze the structural correlation between documentation quality and engineering throughput, using DORA metrics and Accelerate research to quantify the ROI of technical writing.
- [The Developer Trust Hierarchy: Why Practitioner Writing Outranks Marketing Content](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/developer-trust-hierarchy/): I analyze the structural reasons why engineers filter for technical depth and verify-ability, creating a 5-tier hierarchy of developer trust.
- [Technical Content as a Moat: the Long Game for Developer Tools](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/technical-content-as-a-moat-the-long-game-for-developer-tools/): I explain why durable technical content compounds for developer tools, how strong docs and tutorials create distribution, and where teams usually waste the opportunity.
- [How Stripe's Technical Blog Became a Competitive Moat](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/how-stripes-technical-blog-became-a-competitive-moat/): I think Stripe’s technical blog compounds into a moat because it teaches, documents, and shapes developer trust long before a sales conversation starts.
- [Why Devtools Startups Lose Deals Over Bad Docs](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/why-devtools-startups-lose-deals-over-bad-docs/): DevTools startups lose deals long before sales hears the objection. I explain how weak docs break evaluation, trials, and rollout confidence.
- [From Engineer to Technical Writer: What I Kept and What I Left Behind](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/from-engineer-to-technical-writer-what-i-kept-and-what-i-left-behind/): I explain which engineering habits still make me better at technical writing, which ones I had to drop, and how the day-to-day work changed.
- [The Case for Shorter Technical Documentation](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/the-case-for-shorter-technical-documentation/): I think technical docs are often too long for the wrong reasons. Here’s why shorter docs usually work better, and where longer docs still earn their keep.
- [Writing Release Notes That Developers Trust](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/writing-release-notes-that-developers-trust/): Developers trust release notes when they can make upgrade decisions fast. I explain the format, level of detail, and disclosure standard that earns that trust.
- [How to Write a Technical Tutorial That Actually Teaches](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/how-to-write-a-technical-tutorial-that-actually-teaches/): I explain how I structure technical tutorials so readers finish with a working result, fewer hidden assumptions, and less time wasted on broken examples.
- [Developer Onboarding Docs: What Works, What Doesn't](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/developer-onboarding-docs-what-works-what-doesnt/): I’ve seen developer onboarding docs fail for the same reasons over and over. Here’s what actually helps a new engineer ship useful code in week one.
- [Technical Writing for AI Products: The New Rules](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/technical-writing-for-ai-products-the-new-rules/): AI product docs now need prompts, schemas, evals, and version boundaries. I explain the rules I would use to judge a technical writer for an AI company.
- [How to Write a Changelog That Developers Actually Read](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/how-to-write-a-changelog-developers-actually-read/): Most changelogs exist to satisfy a checkbox. Here's what makes developers actually read them, and how to structure entries so breaking changes land before they cause incidents.

## Other Writing

- [Why Your Agent Remembers the Wrong Thing: Memory Attribution Failures](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/memory-attribution-errors/): Memory attribution failures cause AI agents to act on outdated or misassigned context. Here is what I found examining the failure patterns.
- [Shared Memory vs Isolated Memory in Multi-Agent Workflows](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/shared-vs-isolated-memory-multi-agent/): How to choose between shared and isolated memory architectures for multi-agent systems, with trade-offs from production deployments.
- [Memory Versioning and Audit Trails for Regulated AI Agents](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/memory-versioning-and-audit-trails/): If your agent overwrites its memory, you cannot pass a compliance audit. How to build append-only memory versioning and trace agent reasoning.
- [Contextual Compression for Agent Memory: What Stays and What Goes](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/contextual-compression-for-agent-memory/): How agents decide what to keep in memory when context space is finite, and the three compression strategies that actually work.
- [Episodic, Semantic, and Working Memory in AI Agents: A Practical Map](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/episodic-vs-semantic-vs-working-memory-agents/): AI agents juggle three distinct memory types. Getting them wrong is the source of most agent memory failures I see in production.
- [Memory Serialization: How Agents Persist State Across Sessions](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/memory-serialization-between-sessions/): Why agents forget everything on restart, and the serialization patterns that actually solve it
- [Multi-Agent vs Single-Agent Systems: The Real Trade-offs](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/multi-agent-vs-single-agent-tradeoffs/): The decision between one agent and many is not about capability. It is about failure modes, latency, and operational complexity.
- [When to Build an Agent and When to Build a Smarter Assistant](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/agent-vs-ai-assistant/): The difference between an AI agent and a smart assistant comes down to one thing: who drives the loop.
- [Lambda Calculus as AI Reasoning Benchmark](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/lambda-calculus-ai-reasoning-benchmark/): I have used lambda calculus to test whether AI systems can actually reason through composition, or whether they are just pattern-matching their way to plausible outputs.
- [The Anatomy of an Agent Loop: Perceive, Think, Act, Remember](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/agent-loop-anatomy/): The agent loop is not one thing. It is four distinct phases that run in sequence, and understanding each one is how you debug what breaks.
- [Why AI Agents Keep Failing in Production and What the Field Is Doing About It](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/why-ai-agents-keep-failing-in-production/): I have spent two years watching agents fail in production. Here is what I keep seeing and what the field is starting to do about it.
- [The 800ms Barrier: Profiling the Latency Chain of a Real-Time Gemini 3.1 Voice Agent](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/voice-ai-latency-gemini-benchmark/): I built a sub-second latency voice assistant and profiled every millisecond of the Audio-to-Audio request/response loop on a MacBook Air M2. Here is the bottleneck analysis.
- [100ms Vector Search in the Browser: PGlite vs. SQLite-vec Head-to-Head](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/local-wasm-vector-benchmarks/): I benchmarked PGlite and SQLite-vec on a MacBook Air M2 to find the fastest WASM vector database for local-first AI applications.
- [Beyond the Chatbox: Benchmarking Claude Code vs. Gemini CLI for Autonomous Repository Refactoring](https://ninadpathak.com/blog/agentic-cli-benchmarks/): I ran a head-to-head benchmark of Claude Code and Gemini CLI on an M2 Air (16GB), testing their ability to autonomously refactor legacy code and resolve race conditions.

## Glossary

- [Context Engineering](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/context-engineering/): The systematic discipline of designing, structuring, and managing the information provided to a Large Language Model (LLM) to maximize its reasoning capability and accuracy.
- [Agentic Engineering](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/agentic-engineering/): The discipline of designing, orchestrating, and evaluating systems where AI agents autonomously plan and execute multi-step workflows.
- [Flow Engineering](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/flow-engineering/): The practice of designing deterministic, multi-step architectures where an LLM's output is iteratively refined, tested, and corrected through a predefined pipeline.
- [Test-Time Compute (TTC)](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/test-time-compute/): The practice of allocating more computational resources during inference (the 'test time') to allow an AI model to perform internal reasoning, planning, and self-correction before outputting an answer.
- [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/model-context-protocol/): An open-source, standardized protocol that acts as a universal API allowing AI models to securely connect to, read from, and interact with external data sources and tools.
- [Semantic Caching](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/semantic-caching/): An optimization technique that stores previous LLM responses and uses vector similarity to return those cached answers for new queries that have the same semantic meaning, bypassing the LLM entirely.
- [Matryoshka Representation Learning (MRL)](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/matryoshka-representation-learning/): A technique for training embedding models so that the most critical semantic information is front-loaded into the earliest dimensions of the vector, allowing the vector to be truncated to save space without catastrophic accuracy loss.
- [Cross-Encoder Reranking](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/cross-encoder-reranking/): An architecture where an embedding model processes both the user's query and a target document simultaneously through its attention layers to generate a highly precise relevance score.
- [Late Chunking](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/late-chunking/): A RAG optimization where an entire document is processed by the embedding model first to capture global context, and only then split into individual searchable chunks.
- [GraphRAG](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/graphrag/): An advanced retrieval technique that extracts entities and relationships from documents to build a Knowledge Graph, allowing an LLM to answer complex queries that span across multiple disparate documents.
- [Tool Calling](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/tool-calling/): A capability fine-tuned into modern LLMs that allows them to intelligently select an external tool and output the exact JSON arguments required to execute it.
- [PagedAttention](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/pagedattention/): An attention algorithm inspired by operating system memory paging that non-contiguously stores an LLM's KV cache, dramatically increasing the throughput of AI servers.
- [KV Cache Eviction](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/kv-cache-eviction/): Algorithms that selectively delete less important tokens from an LLM's Key-Value cache during inference to prevent GPU memory exhaustion during long conversations.
- [Semantic Chunking](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/semantic-chunking/): A method of splitting text for vector databases that evaluates the embedding distance between sentences, ensuring that logically coherent thoughts are kept together in a single chunk.
- [Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE)](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/hypothetical-document-embeddings-hyde/): A zero-shot retrieval technique where a user's question is first answered by an LLM (hallucinating a hypothetical document), and that hallucinated answer is embedded and used to search the vector database.
- [Self-Querying Retrieval](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/self-querying-retrieval/): An intelligent retrieval mechanism where an LLM parses a user's natural language query to automatically extract exact metadata filters (like dates or authors) while simultaneously performing a semantic vector search.
- [Bi-Encoder Architecture](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/bi-encoder/): A neural network architecture where two separate inputs (like a query and a document) are passed through an embedding model independently to create two distinct vectors that can be compared mathematically.
- [Product Quantization (PQ)](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/product-quantization/): A highly efficient lossy compression algorithm used in vector databases to drastically reduce the memory footprint of embeddings while maintaining fast nearest-neighbor search capabilities.
- [Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW)](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/hierarchical-navigable-small-world-hnsw/): A graph-based algorithm that organizes vector embeddings into multiple layers, enabling sub-millisecond Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search across billions of data points.
- [ReAct Prompting](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/react-prompting/): An agentic framework that strictly interleaves internal reasoning (Chain of Thought) with external actions (Tool Calling), allowing an LLM to solve complex, multi-step problems autonomously.
- [Plan-and-Solve Framework](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/plan-and-solve-framework/): An agentic architecture where a complex objective is broken down into a complete, sequential list of sub-tasks before any actual execution or tool calling begins.
- [Speculative Decoding](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/speculative-decoding/): An inference optimization technique that accelerates text generation by using a small, fast 'draft' model to predict multiple upcoming tokens, which are then verified simultaneously by the main, larger model.
- [Agentic Router](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/agentic-router/): A lightweight classifier model or programmatic logic layer that analyzes an incoming user query and routes it to the specific AI agent, tool, or LLM best suited to handle it.
- [JSON Mode vs. Structured Outputs](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/json-mode-vs-structured-outputs/): JSON Mode heavily biases an LLM to produce valid JSON syntax, whereas Structured Outputs mathematically constrain the model's token generation to guarantee the output perfectly matches a specific JSON Schema.
- [DSPy](https://ninadpathak.com/glossary/dspy/): An open-source framework developed by Stanford that replaces manual prompt engineering with programmatic compilation, allowing developers to write declarative logic that automatically optimizes the prompts sent to an LLM.

## Work & Case Studies

- [50+ Developer Guides That Engineers Actually Use](https://ninadpathak.com/work/centus/): Created 50+ implementation-level localization guides for Centus across major frameworks, with engineer reviews moving straight to publish.
- [Zero to 50,000 Monthly Organic Visits](https://ninadpathak.com/work/delightchat/): Built DelightChat's content program from zero and grew it to 50,000 monthly organic visits by targeting real eCommerce support problems.
- [7,000 to 450,000 Monthly Visits, A 64x Traffic Multiplier](https://ninadpathak.com/work/kiwisizing/): Built a programmatic SEO system for Kiwi Sizing that grew organic traffic from 7,000 to 450,000 monthly visits without turning the site into template spam.
- [From 487 to 203,000 Monthly Visits, And $34K More MRR](https://ninadpathak.com/work/linuxfordevices/): Built LinuxForDevices' content and distribution system from scratch, growing traffic from 487 to 203,400 monthly visits and adding $34K in MRR.
- [Scaling Mem0 from 24k to 45k Monthly Visits in 60 Days](https://ninadpathak.com/work/mem0/): Managed a team of 13 writers and implemented a fully automated content engine for Mem0, increasing organic traffic by 50% within two months of joining.
